QF.id: Clubbing, Set-Off and Carry Forward of Losses_19

question.text: Mr. Rohan, an investment adviser, has a client who incurred a Long-Term Capital Loss of Rs.4,00,000 in FY 2024-25 (timely return filed). In FY 2025-26, the client has Short-Term Capital Gain of Rs.3,00,000 and Long-Term Capital Gain of Rs.2,00,000 but no other capital transactions. How should Rohan advise the client to utilize the brought-forward LTCL?

answer.text: Set off Rs.3,00,000 LTCL against STCG and Rs.1,00,000 against LTCG — fully utilizing the LTCL
Set off Rs.2,00,000 LTCL against LTCG only; carry forward the remaining Rs.2,00,000 LTCL; STCG of Rs.3,00,000 remains fully taxable
Set off Rs.4,00,000 LTCL against combined capital gains of Rs.5,00,000; net taxable gain = Rs.1,00,000
LTCL from previous year cannot be carried forward; it expired in FY 2024-25 itself

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Brought-forward LTCL can ONLY be set off against LTCG — not against STCG. Available LTCG = Rs.2,00,000. So Rs.2,00,000 of LTCL is set off against LTCG. Remaining LTCL = Rs.4,00,000 - Rs.2,00,000 = Rs.2,00,000 (carried forward, available for 8 years from FY 2024-25). STCG of Rs.3,00,000 remains fully taxable. **The Trap:** Option A is a very strong distractor — it seems logical to use the full LTCL against any capital gain, but the law restricts LTCL to LTCG only. Option C makes the same error. **Cross-Exam Context:** This LTCL restriction is one of the most tested concepts in NISM X-B taxation modules. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Plan tax-loss harvesting at year-end. If a client has expiring LTCL, consider booking LTCG on winners to absorb it before the 8-year window closes.
